title: #eng-runtime: 1739305589
preview:
Noah Patel: heads up for quant profile library runtime wiring — a few concerns I want to sync on:
1) determinism (same request+route should resolve same profile every time)
2) cache interactions (prefix/KV cache + batching) w/ mixed quant modes
3) traces/logs: how do we *prove* profile applied vs fallback

Sofia Ivanova: +1 esp on determinism. if control plane returns `recommended` alias and that changes under us, we’ll have "same config" but different kernels/weights day-to-day.

Jordan Lee: determinism requirement from platform side: resolved settings must be deterministic per route config snapshot. For v1 we should treat `quantization_profile_ref` as pinned (profile_id + version), not a moving alias.

Noah Patel: ok so runtime should only see a fully-resolved `profile_id@version`? (or `disabled`)

Jordan Lee: yep. policy engine resolves route cfg -> concrete profile ref. if alias exists at all it should be resolved at write-time, not request-time.

Mateo Alvarez: strongly prefer that. runtime hot path shouldn’t have to interpret aliases.

Omar El-Sayed: cache stuff: we need to make sure we don’t share KV cache entries across quant mode boundaries. if we have prefix caching keyed just on prompt hash + model_id, enabling INT8 on route would start reusing FP16 KV from earlier traffic :grimacing:

Grace Kim: yup, and also attention kernels behave differently w/ some act quant paths. key must include at least `quant_profile_id` (or "baseline") + maybe some kernel selection hash.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel selection is a function of (gpu sku, seq len, batch shape, quant mode). If we switch quant, the selected kernel path can change. Agree cache key needs `quant_profile_id` and probably `quant_mode` is redundant if profile id is present.

Noah Patel: current cache key (simplified) is:
`cache_key = model_id + tokenizer_rev + prompt_prefix_hash + max_new_tokens_bucket`
We can add `quant_profile_id` and `weights_rev`.

Omar El-Sayed: also watch out for "baseline" not being a real profile id. I suggest we normalize to `quant_profile_id=none` and include that string in the key so we don’t collide.

Sofia Ivanova: batching: if continuous batching mixes requests with different quant profiles on same replica, are we going to be ok? most implementations assume same weights resident.

Hiro Tanaka: mixing profiles in a batch is a no-go if it implies different weights / different quantized weight pack. If profiles are per-model variant and you load one profile per worker, then batch scheduler must bucket by `quant_profile_id`.

Grace Kim: +1. some profiles may change weight packing layout too.

Noah Patel: so options:
A) worker pins to one profile per model variant (baseline or a given profile) and scheduler routes accordingly
B) allow dynamic switching but then you thrash weight loads and blow latency

Mateo Alvarez: A for v1. treat profile selection like model variant selection. runtime: route -> (model_variant, quant_profile_id) -> pool key.

Sofia Ivanova: ok then queue partitioning by pool key. we already do that for model variants.

Omar El-Sayed: memory impact: if we have both baseline + quant workers live, KV cache memory behavior differs (int8 activations can reduce compute but KV often stays fp16/bf16). we should confirm we’re not assuming kv dtype changes.

Hiro Tanaka: current plan: quantization profile affects weights (and maybe activation quant in matmuls), but KV cache dtype remains bf16. so kv memory doesn’t drop materially; decode perf might improve. need to document.

Noah Patel: traces/logs: what’s the best way to surface profile application? We need to answer "did this request run under profile X or fallback?" quickly.

Caleb Johnson: tracing side: we can tag spans with attributes. suggestion:
- `llm.quant.profile_id` (string; "none" for baseline)
- `llm.quant.applied` (true/false)
- `llm.quant.resolution` ("applied" | "fallback" | "disabled")
- `llm.quant.fallback_reason` (enum string; e.g. "incompatible_gpu" "seq_len_exceeded" "kill_switch" "artifact_load_failed")
Also log annotation on request log for sampling.

Logan Wright: metrics too pls. if you emit `fallback_rate{route,model,profile_id,reason}` we can build alerting. but traces are essential for debugging single request IDs.

Grace Kim: for fallback reason, please include kernel guardrails too (e.g. "unsupported_kernel_path") so we can triage quickly.

Noah Patel: where do we decide fallback? policy engine may already do compatibility checks, but runtime can still fail to load artifact.

Jordan Lee: plan is: policy does deterministic compatibility (gpu class, max ctx, model family). runtime still needs runtime-guardrails (artifact fetch failure, checksum mismatch, unexpected precision) -> fallback with reason.

Mateo Alvarez: we should make sure fallback is *single hop* — don’t want loops like: apply profile -> fails -> retry policy -> re-resolves same profile -> fails again.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. runtime should have local "profile load failed" cache with TTL so we don’t hammer registry.

Noah Patel: good call. Implementation thought:
- on request: resolved_profile_ref comes from router
- runtime attempts `ensure_profile_loaded(ref)`
- if error: mark `ref` unhealthy for (say) 60s, emit event, then run baseline weights

Omar El-Sayed: and caches should record which profile ran. otherwise prefix cache might still store entries under the "requested" profile id even if fallback happened.

Noah Patel: yup, cache key should use *effective* profile id (after fallback). So if fallback occurs, set `effective_quant_profile_id=none`.

Caleb Johnson: for trace tagging, use effective too, and add requested separately:
- `llm.quant.requested_profile_id`
- `llm.quant.effective_profile_id`

Grace Kim: +1, otherwise you can’t tell why it didn’t apply.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel selection logs: can we include a compact `kernel_path` tag? even a short enum helps (e.g. `flashattn_v3_int8w` vs `flashattn_v3_fp16`).

Noah Patel: we can put it in debug logs, not sure about always-on trace tags (cardinality). maybe a sampled event.

Logan Wright: agree re cardinality. put kernel path behind debug sampling; but profile_id is ok.

Sofia Ivanova: another determinism angle: if profile artifacts stored in registry change in-place, runtime could load different bits under same id. do we have signing/hash?

Mateo Alvarez: profiles must be immutable by (id, version). registry should serve content-addressed artifacts, or at least enforce immutability w/ checksum validation.

Jordan Lee: registry team is adding signing + versioned artifact storage. runtime validates checksum before using.

Noah Patel: ok. I’ll add to runtime design doc:
- pool key includes profile_id@version
- scheduler buckets by pool key; no mixed-profile batches
- prefix/KV cache key includes effective profile id
- trace tags for requested/effective + resolution + fallback reason
- unhealthy cache to prevent thundering herd on repeated load failures

Omar El-Sayed: please call out interaction w/ prefix cache invalidation: when toggling profile on a route, old prefix cache entries stay but are keyed differently, so safe (but cache hit rate resets). that’s expected.

Sofia Ivanova: and mention latency hit on first request after enable due to profile load. can we prewarm?

Noah Patel: yeah: control plane / console can trigger "preload" by hitting a warmup endpoint or we can lazy-load with a clear warning. v1 probably lazy + doc.

Caleb Johnson: last request: make sure request logs have a single field too. Something like `quant_profile_id=<effective>` and `quant_resolution=<...>` so oncall can grep.

Noah Patel: ack. I’ll coordinate with logging/tracing. thanks all.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: please drop the draft in #eng-runtime once you’ve updated. want to sanity check before we commit to API shapes.

Noah Patel: will do.

title: #architecture: 1738765432
preview:
Priya Natarajan: quick RFC follow-up — thinking about making rollout metadata intentionally minimal on the wire and doing client-delta probing from the console. TL;DR: small pings for compatibility, server-side enrichment for ops. tradeoffs?
Ben Cho: +1 to minimalism, but worried about post-rollback debugging. we should keep at least: model, variant, pin_reason, expires_at/soft_ttl. otherwise ops blind.
Mateo Alvarez: +1. also how do we distinguish deprecation vs soft pin? suggest a compact enum like retire_hint: 'none'|'warn'|'soft_retire'|'hard_retire' so clients can surface warnings.
Priya Natarajan: one option. my proposal: wire payload stays tiny, something like:
```json
{"model":"redwood/llama-70b","variant":"q4o","pin_mode":"soft","expires_at":"2026-04-01T12:00:00Z"}
```
control plane enriches with deprecation hints, computed priority, origin, etc., only when a client opts in or for console UI.
Lina (eng-bot): deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 pushed to staging, schema validation tests: 45/50 passed, failing cases are optional fields parsing.
Ben Cho: practical concern — older SDKs will ignore expires_at. we need server-side grace/fallback window (eg: 72h default) + tenant override. otherwise pins could silently linger.
Mateo Alvarez: agreed. also when overlapping pins occur we need deterministic resolver rules (priority > created_ts > origin). should we encode 'priority' on-wire or keep it server-computed?
Priya Natarajan: prefer server-computed priority to keep wire small. but we can include a small optional debug view: origin and computed_priority when clients opt-in to richer payloads. that keeps default clients lean.
Ben Cho: sounds good. propose two small optional fields: "origin" (string) and "computed_priority" (int). optional, so normal clients ignore them. keeps debugging feasible.
Mateo Alvarez: telemetry-wise we should attach a tiny "delta_probe" sample: {last_seen_version, last_probe_ts, sdk_version}. very small, helps migration metrics.
Priya Natarajan: deprecation semantics need to be explicit in ops playbook: warn -> console alert only; soft_retire -> attempt autoreplace with compatible variant after N failures; hard_retire -> block new pins and trigger pager. include tenant/SLA exemptions for autoreplace.
Ben Cho: add constraint: autoreplace must respect tenant pin overrides and SLA tier — premium tenants opt-out of auto-migrations.
Mateo Alvarez: question for private installs: if control plane enriches metadata, should we sign that enrichment so on-host components can trust it?
Priya Natarajan: yes. for Private we should sign control-plane-issued enrichment payloads. hosted infra can rely on internal auth, but signature optional field for private installs.
Ben Cho: action items: (1) draft minimal wire schema doc + example payloads, (2) define server enrichment fields + signing rules, (3) implement grace window/fallback behavior, (4) add delta_probe telemetry proto, (5) update SDKs to parse optional debug fields.
Priya Natarajan: I'll draft the PR for the wire schema + example payloads and ping @Mateo Alvarez and @Ben Cho for review. thanks all.

Priya Natarajan: I'll draft the PR for the wire schema + example payloads and ping @Mateo Alvarez and @Ben Cho for review. thanks all.

title: #eng-ml: 1733337777
preview:
Sara Li: Quick snapshot from last night's micro-bringup for nova-lite-9b. Ran int6/int8 and a hybrid-fallback variant. TL;DR: int6 cuts cost ~42% vs fp16 but we saw a small hit on long-form summarization recall (esp >800 tokens).

Dan Cho: can you paste the perf/cost numbers?

Sara Li: Yep — small table below (rough averages from 10k sample). Note latency is p95 on our test harness.

Sara Li: ```
model                     | cost/unit | latency p95 | long-recall @800+
nova-lite-9b-fp16         | $1.00     | 210ms       | 0.82
nova-lite-9b-int8         | $0.62     | 230ms       | 0.80
nova-lite-9b-int6         | $0.58     | 260ms       | 0.76
hybrid(int6->fp16 fallback)| $0.63     | 215ms avg    | 0.80
```

Maya R.: That p95 jump on int6 is concerning for chat flows. where's the latency coming from? kv-cache misses?

Mateo Alvarez: int6 currently forces a slightly different kernel path that doesn't use optimized fused attention for seq>512 — we hit a slower fallback for long sequences. Also cache alignment for our batching is less effective (more padding).

Kevin Patel: Product (Priya) — for GTN, do we target short QA/snippets or long summaries? That changes the recommendation.

Priya Singh: GTN will run mixed workloads but heavy on multi-turn conversation and occasional long-form summarization (~15% of requests >700 tokens). Latency SLO is 250ms p95. Cost sensitivity moderate.

Dan Cho: Given that, pure int6 misses the latency SLO and hurts recall. hybrid looks attractive — cost ~37% savings vs fp16 and p95 ~215ms which meets SLO.

Sara Li: Agree. Also ran a quick quality regression check vs our baseline prompt set. int6 had a 4.5% drop in Rouge-L on long docs, int8 1.5% drop, hybrid ~1.6% drop (because fallback triggers on long contexts).

Priya Singh: Product priority: keep quality >0.80 recall and hit latency SLO. Hybrid seems acceptable. Any operational caveats?

Mateo Alvarez: Implementation notes: need routing flag + fast route to fp16 for contexts with >512 tokens. That requires a lightweight context-length classifier at router (cheap). Also need to tune padding/batch heuristics to avoid p95 inflation.

Kevin Patel: Risk: fallback increases cross-model traffic which complicates cost accounting and cache warmstart. But unit econ still better than fp16.

Sara Li: Suggested rollout plan: 1) Canary hybrid in us-east on 10% traffic; 2) collect quality and tail latency slices; 3) if long-doc recall stays within -1.5% of baseline, expand to 50%; 4) enable autoscale adjustments for bursty sessions.

Dan Cho: sounds good. Can we add a small eval hook that raises alert if fallback rate >12% or p95 >250ms?

Mateo Alvarez: yep, I'll add tracing + metric (fallback_rate, p95_by_seq_bucket). ops-bot: create rollout ticket?

ops-bot: Created ticket RW-1428: "Hybrid nova-lite-9b bringup (canary + metrics)" assigned to Mateo, Sara, Dan.

Priya Singh: Approved from product. Please include a one-pager for GTN explaining UX expectations if rare quality regressions happen (graceful retry to fp16).

Sara Li: Will do. I'll post the canary checklist and a short snippet for the router rule later today.

Kevin Patel: Final recommendation for GTN: roll hybrid int6->fp16 with conservative fallback threshold and monitor long-doc quality. Approve? :thumbsup:

Priya Singh: :thumbsup:

Maya R.: :thumbsup:

Dan Cho: let's set a two-week canary window and reconvene with slice stats.

Sara Li: Thanks all — launching canary today EOD. I'll drop links to dashboards when live.

Mateo Alvarez: I'll instrument the metrics and alert thresholds.

Priya Singh: Appreciate the fast turnaround.

Sara Li: Link to supporting eval notebook: https://redwood.internal/notes/nova-lite-9b-eval (internal)

title: #eng-platform: 1726039999
preview:
Alice Chen: Quick proposal — move the rerank response score field from a raw float to a compact integer bucket (int8/int16) + optional normalized float when requested. Goals: reduce payload & telemetry cardinality, make dashboards stable, and enable cheaper storage for long eval traces. Draft below.

Alice Chen: Current shape:```json
{
  "candidates": [
    {"id": "u1", "score": 0.873246},
    {"id": "u2", "score": 0.512345}
  ]
}
```

Alice Chen: Proposed shape (default compact):```json
{
  "candidates": [
    {"id": "u1", "score_bucket": 201},
    {"id": "u2", "score_bucket": 118}
  ],
  "score_meta": {"bucket_bits": 8, "scale": 255}
}
```

Alice Chen: If client asks for high-precision (query param `?score_format=raw`) we keep the old float `score` for compatibility.

Mateo Alvarez: How are we defining buckets? linear or log? 8 bits feels small for some dense models. :thinking:

Alice Chen: Good Q — plan is linear mapping to [0,1] by default, with `scale` metadata so you can reconstruct: score = bucket/scale. For tails we can reserve special buckets (0 = absent, 255 = +inf) — will document.

Priya Singh: How does this affect quota/billing? Right now we charge per candidate scored unit. If we change field names, will telemetry still map to units?

SREbot: Telemetry note: event size drops ~30% in our POC (smaller payload + fewer float precision diffs). Billing plumbing uses request.meta.candidate_count, unaffected by this change. But downstream ETL (score histograms) will need new decode path.

Dan O'Neill: Routing question — some product teams rely on model affinity to pick the specialized reorderer. We should send `X-Model-Affinity` and a `score_format` hint in the routing header so Dedicated/Private routers can pick the right binary. E.g.:`X-Rerank-Score-Format: compact-v1`

Mateo Alvarez: If the compact format is the default surface, how do we run a canary? Need console UX to toggle per-route behavior and show an A/B distribution of score buckets vs raw.

ProductBot: From console/docs POV: show both representations during rollout. Add a small histogram tile that displays bucket occupancy and a precision delta chart (compact vs raw) for the canary cohort. UX should default to "show normalized score" for non-expert users.

Alice Chen: Compatibility plan: server accepts `score` (float) or `score_bucket` (int). For outgoing hosted API responses, default to compact for new tenants. Add `accept-score-format` header to opt-in raw. Console will have rollout toggle under routes -> rerank -> scoring format (0-100% canary).

Priya Singh: Telemetry schema change request: add `score_representation` to rerank_request events (`compact` | `raw`) and include `bucket_bits` & `scale` in the event metadata. That way dashboards can auto-decode.

SREbot: Also add a new telemetry metric `rerank.bucket_entropy` to detect degenerate bucketization (e.g., everything falling in top 2 buckets). Alert if entropy < 0.2 for 1h.

Dan O'Neill: About fallback — if a model variant can't produce buckets (older model weights), router should fallback to `format=raw` and we must record an explicit `format_fallback` tag. No silent coercion.

Mateo Alvarez: Implementation detail: runtime should expose `model.capabilities.score_bucketing = {bits: 8, supported: true}` so router can make decisions without probing. Also add a quick smoke test that compares rank order between raw vs compact for sample inputs to catch regressions.

Alice Chen: Sounds like consensus. Next steps: 1) eng-platform to land compact wire format + server acceptance for both shapes (owner: Mateo) 2) eng-runtime to export model.capabilities (owner: Dan) 3) telemetry to add `score_representation` & `bucket_entropy` (owner: Priya) 4) console to add rollout toggle + histogram card (owner: Product). We'll do a 2-week canary at 5/20 -> 6/3 on internal tenants then expand.

ProductBot: I'll add a small copy doc for the console toggle and the help tooltip: "Compact scores reduce payload and stabilize dashboards; enable only after validating rank parity."

Alice Chen: OK I'll open the RFC PR with the JSON shapes and a migration checklist. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

Priya Singh: +1, will coordinate telemetry schema change.

Dan O'Neill: I'll stub the capability field in runtime today.

Mateo Alvarez: Starting the wire-format patch this afternoon.

SREbot: Scheduled alert rule draft created: `rerank.bucket_entropy < 0.2` -> pager if >60m.

Alice Chen: Closing the thread — next sync after RFC review.
text:

Goals: reduce payload & telemetry cardinality, make dashboards stable, and enable cheaper storage for long eval traces. Draft below.

    {"id": "u2", "score": 0.512345}

}

We'll do a 2-week canary at 5/20 -> 6/3 on internal tenants then expand.

ProductBot: I'll add a small copy doc for the console toggle and the help tooltip: "Compact scores reduce payload and stabilize dashboards; enable only after validating rank parity."

Alice Chen: OK I'll open the RFC PR with the JSON shapes and a migration checklist. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

Dan O'Neill: I'll stub the capability field in runtime today.

title: #new-hires: 1725000000
preview:
Ellen Park: Hi all, I accepted the "Orientation: Nov 30" invite but my calendar shows it at 9am instead of 10am. I'm in Dublin. Is the invite wrong?
Mateo Alvarez: Hi @Ellen — did you get the regional repeat? We have both EMEA and Global invitations, sometimes both land in calendars. Which event title does it show exactly?
Ellen Park: It says 'Orientation: Redwood Welcome (Global)' and another says 'Orientation: Redwood Welcome (EMEA)'. The Global one is 09:00 and EMEA is 10:00. Which one should I follow?
Sana Rao: Keep the EMEA one — that's the canonical session for Dublin. We always recommend deleting the Global one to avoid duplicates.
Ellen Park: Got it. Any chance the ICS is using UTC and showing differently because DST just ended?
Mateo Alvarez: We specified times in UTC in the calendar send, but some clients render both UTC and local offsets. For Nov 30 the correct local time is 10:00 Europe/Dublin (GMT). We'll update the ICS to include TZID=Europe/Dublin to reduce client glitching.
OnboardingBot: FYI Recording will be uploaded to https://files.redwood.ai/onboarding/fall2024 within 2 hours. Reminder scheduled for 24h and 30m before EMEA session.
Ellen Park: Thanks — I deleted the Global one and kept EMEA. Looks correct now (10:00). See you there! :wave:
